analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 11 finding(s) from 12 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: DMI/JTAG access appears not to be mediated by the visible JTAG key/authentication condition. The TAP selects DMI access solely from the JTAG instruction, TDO shifts DMI response data for DMIACCESS, and dm_csrs reports authenticated=1 with a comment that no authentication is implemented. dmi_jtag contains jtag_key/pass/pass_chk signals, but the observed request/response path to dmi_cdc is not shown as being guarded by pass_chk.
findings.vulnerability_category: Read/write DMI registers and shift DMI responses out over TDO. / DMI registers in dm_csrs and downstream debug CSRs/SBA controls. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: External JTAG/SimJTAG requester via tck/tms/td_i and DMI requester. Operation: Read/write DMI registers and shift DMI responses out over TDO. Object: DMI registers in dm_csrs and downstream debug CSRs/SBA controls. Expected guard: DMI/debug-register operations should be mediated by jtag_key/pass_chk or equivalent authorization before dmi_req_valid_o issues requests or dmi_resp data is shifted out. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag_tap asserts dmi_access_o on DMIACCESS instruction and selects dmi_tdo_i onto TDO for DMIACCESS; dm_csrs hardwires dmstatus.authenticated=1 and comments 'no authentication implemented'. The dmi_cdc connects dmi_req_valid to dmi_req_valid_o without an observed pass_chk gate. jtag_key is only observed being copied to pass on reset. Path: SimJTAG/external JTAG pins -> dmi_jtag_tap DMIACCESS -> dmi_jtag dmi_cdc -> dm_top/dm_csrs; response returns through dmi_resp_i/dmi_tdo to td_o. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthenticated external JTAG traffic can issue DMI reads/writes and receive responses, enabling access to debug CSRs, halt control, and SBA paths.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The earlier combinational block in dmi_jtag where pass_chk may be computed was truncated; however, the observed downstream/authenticated status evidence does not show a complete pass_chk gate on DMI requests/responses.

findings.summary: External DMI writes can drive halt/resume/debug command state without an observed authentication guard. DMControl writes are accepted from DMI and haltreq/resumereq outputs are generated directly from dmcontrol_q for the selected hart; dm_mem then receives haltreq/resumereq/cmd_valid and produces debug_req_o.
findings.vulnerability_category: Halt, resume, enter debug mode, issue abstract debug commands. / haltreq_o, resumereq_o, debug_req_o, dmcontrol/debug command state, csr_regfile debug_mode_q/dpc/dscratch. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: External debugger via JTAG/DMI. Operation: Halt, resume, enter debug mode, issue abstract debug commands. Object: haltreq_o, resumereq_o, debug_req_o, dmcontrol/debug command state, csr_regfile debug_mode_q/dpc/dscratch. Expected guard: External debug halt/resume and debug mode entry should require authorized debug/DMI access plus legal dmactive/hart-selection state. Observed behavior: DMControl write case assigns dmcontrol_d from dmi_req_i.data; haltreq_o[selected_hart] and resumereq_o[selected_hart] are directly assigned from dmcontrol_q bits. dm_top connects these outputs to dm_mem, whose debug_req_o drives core debug request. No pass_chk/jtag_key guard or unavailable/hartsel legality guard is visible on these assignments. Path: JTAG/DMI -> dm_csrs DMControl/Command writes -> haltreq/resumereq/cmd_valid -> dm_top dm_mem -> debug_req_o -> csr_regfile debug_req_i/debug_mode_q. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An unauthenticated external debugger could halt/resume the hart or request debug entry, changing processor execution state and exposing/controling debug-mode resources.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full dm_mem internals are not provided, so exact debug_req_o generation is inferred from dm_top connectivity and csr_regfile behavior.

findings.summary: Debug System Bus Access can be configured through DMI and connected to an AXI master without observed address/range/access-policy mediation. dm_csrs exposes sbaddress/sbdata/control signals, and dm_top connects them to dm_sba and axi_m_req_o; only dmactive_i is visible on dm_sba, not a protected-range policy.
findings.vulnerability_category: Debug-initiated system bus read/write, read-on-address/data, autoincrement. / AXI master address space reachable through dm_sba, including memory/peripherals if routed there. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: External debugger/DMI requester using SBA registers. Operation: Debug-initiated system bus read/write, read-on-address/data, autoincrement. Object: AXI master address space reachable through dm_sba, including memory/peripherals if routed there. Expected guard: SBA should be restricted by debug authorization plus address/range/access-size policy covering sbaddress, sbdata, autoincrement, read-on-data/address. Observed behavior: dm_csrs drives sbautoincrement_o, sbreadonaddr_o, sbreadondata_o, sbaccess_o, sbdata_o, and sbaddress_o from CSR state; dm_top wires these into dm_sba, which drives axi_m_req_o. The observed wiring shows no range filter or privilege/security attribute check before AXI master issuance. Path: DMI -> dm_csrs SBA CSRs -> dm_top sbaddress/sbdata/control wires -> dm_sba -> axi_m_req_o / axi_m_resp_i -> SoC fabric. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If DMI is reachable, debug SBA may read/write arbitrary bus locations, bypassing normal core privilege/PMP/MMU checks and potentially reaching protected memory or peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: dm_sba internals and full SoC address map/routing are not provided; range-policy absence is based on visible connections and lack of guard evidence.

findings.summary: Some busy-state protections exist for abstract command data/command/autoexec writes, but DMControl updates, response FIFO stale data handling, and dmactive/deactivation interactions are not fully guarded. DMControl is assigned from DMI data even while operations may be busy, and the response FIFO is reset only by dmi_rst_ni with flush_i tied low.
findings.vulnerability_category: Configure/update debug command, DMControl, SBA state, and reset/debug-active state during active operations. / dmcontrol_q, command/data/progbuf/abstractauto, sbaddr/sbdata/sbcs, response FIFO, cmdbusy/sbbusy, dmactive_o/ndmreset_o. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: DMI requester. Operation: Configure/update debug command, DMControl, SBA state, and reset/debug-active state during active operations. Object: dmcontrol_q, command/data/progbuf/abstractauto, sbaddr/sbdata/sbcs, response FIFO, cmdbusy/sbbusy, dmactive_o/ndmreset_o. Expected guard: Writes affecting selected hart, command/SBA, or reset/debug-active state should be blocked or error while busy; reset/active transitions should clear or invalidate stale responses and permissions. Observed behavior: Data, AbstractCS, Command, AbstractAuto, and ProgBuf writes include cmdbusy checks/errors. However, DMControl writes assign dmcontrol_d unconditionally from dmi_req_i.data. The response FIFO has flush_i tied to 1'b0 and reset only by dmi_rst_ni, not by dmactive clear; dmcontrol_q deactivation only resets selected fields in sequential logic after line 517. Path: DMI writes -> dm_csrs write case -> dmcontrol/command/SBA/register/FIFO state -> dm_mem or dm_sba operations. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A requester may change halt/resume/reset/active/hart selection during active operations or observe stale queued responses after debug deactivation, causing authorization/state confusion and possible stale data exposure.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The truncated portions around SBA register write cases and complete dmactive reset block may contain additional checks not visible here.

findings.summary: CSR read/write decode exposes and updates machine, supervisor, and debug CSRs without visible privilege checks based on CSR address privilege bits/current mode. The read mux returns machine/supervisor/debug registers directly, and the write case updates debug/machine CSRs directly; no guard on priv_lvl_q/CSR privilege appears in the inspected decode snippets.
findings.vulnerability_category: CSR read/write through csr_addr_i/csr_wdata_i/csr_op_i. / Machine/supervisor/debug CSRs including mtvec, medeleg, mideleg, mie/mip, mstatus, satp, dpc, dscratch, cache-control CSRs. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Core software executing CSR instruction. Operation: CSR read/write through csr_addr_i/csr_wdata_i/csr_op_i. Object: Machine/supervisor/debug CSRs including mtvec, medeleg, mideleg, mie/mip, mstatus, satp, dpc, dscratch, cache-control CSRs. Expected guard: CSR operations should enforce privilege based on CSR address/current mode and raise access exceptions rather than exposing/updating protected CSRs. Observed behavior: Read cases return debug, supervisor, and machine CSRs without shown privilege checks. Write cases update DCSR/DPC/DSCRATCH and machine CSR state without shown privilege checks; default alone sets update_access_exception for unrecognized CSRs. priv_lvl_o assignment also maps debug_mode_q or umode_i to machine privilege, increasing concern. Path: csr_op_i/csr_addr_i -> csr_op_logic -> csr_read_write read/write cases -> CSR state/output csr_rdata_o/csr_exception_o. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Lower-privilege software may read or modify protected CSRs, altering trap routing, interrupt controls, debug state, cache controls, and memory-protection policy.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Earlier/later code that might set csr_exception_o from CSR address privilege bits was not fully shown; no such guard was present in inspected decode snippets.

findings.summary: Debug-only CSRs and DRET are not visibly guarded by debug_mode_q. DCSR/DPC/DSCRATCH reads and writes appear as ordinary CSR cases, and DRET sets dret regardless of debug_mode_q in csr_op_logic; epc_o then selects dpc_q when dret is asserted.
findings.vulnerability_category: Read/write debug CSRs and exit via DRET. / dpc_q, dscratch0_q, dscratch1_q, dcsr_q, debug_mode_q, single_step_o. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Core software executing CSR/DRET instruction; external debugger indirectly through debug state. Operation: Read/write debug CSRs and exit via DRET. Object: dpc_q, dscratch0_q, dscratch1_q, dcsr_q, debug_mode_q, single_step_o. Expected guard: Debug CSRs should be readable/writable only in debug mode or through authorized debug mechanisms; DRET should be legal only when debug_mode_q is true. Observed behavior: Read cases return CSR_DCSR, CSR_DPC, CSR_DSCRATCH0, and CSR_DSCRATCH1 without a debug_mode_q condition. Write cases update DCSR, DPC, and DSCRATCH registers without a debug_mode_q condition. DRET operation sets dret=1 with no debug_mode_q guard; epc_o uses dpc_q if dret. Path: CSR instruction -> csr_regfile read/write/dret decode -> debug CSR registers/debug return path -> epc_o/set_debug_pc_o/debug_mode_q. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Non-debug software can potentially read/modify debug return PC/scratch/control state or execute DRET to redirect control flow via dpc_q.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No full illegal-instruction exception logic for DRET/Debug CSR privilege was visible in the provided snippets.

findings.summary: Memory translation and access-policy CSRs appear to be writable/readable through the same unguarded CSR decode, and outputs directly drive MMU policy. The inspected snippets show SATP readable and mstatus/machine policy fields output directly, without visible lower-privilege denial.
findings.vulnerability_category: Configure memory translation and privilege policy via CSR writes/read and return-from-trap mode updates. / satp_ppn_o, asid_o, en_translation_o, en_ld_st_translation_o, sum_o, mxr_o, tvm_o, tw_o, tsr_o, mprv/mstatus state. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Core software at current privilege level. Operation: Configure memory translation and privilege policy via CSR writes/read and return-from-trap mode updates. Object: satp_ppn_o, asid_o, en_translation_o, en_ld_st_translation_o, sum_o, mxr_o, tvm_o, tw_o, tsr_o, mprv/mstatus state. Expected guard: Only sufficiently privileged software should configure satp and mstatus privilege-policy fields; lower-privilege attempts should raise exceptions and mode transitions should update effective permissions safely. Observed behavior: CSR_SATP is returned by the read mux; mstatus-derived outputs directly drive sum_o/mxr_o/tvm_o/tw_o/tsr_o and translation enable. The inspected CSR write decode does not show privilege checks before protected CSR updates, and priv_lvl_o is forced to machine when umode_i is asserted. Path: CSR instruction -> csr_regfile decode/state update -> satp_q/mstatus_q -> MMU/access-policy outputs. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Lower-privilege code may disable/alter translation or weaken memory access policy, bypassing intended memory protection and isolation.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Specific SATP write case and complete privilege exception logic were not included in the provided observations.

findings.summary: Trap vector, delegation, interrupt, EPC, and cause CSRs are exposed by CSR read/write decode without visible privilege enforcement. Machine trap/interrupt CSR write cases update state directly, and trap_vector_base_o/epc_o are driven from those registers and return-operation state.
findings.vulnerability_category: Configure trap/interrupt routing and exception return targets; execute mret/sret. / mtvec_q, stvec_q, medeleg_q, mideleg_q, mie_q, mip_q, mepc_q, sepc_q, mcause_q, scause_q, trap_vector_base_o, epc_o. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Core software executing CSR or trap-return instruction. Operation: Configure trap/interrupt routing and exception return targets; execute mret/sret. Object: mtvec_q, stvec_q, medeleg_q, mideleg_q, mie_q, mip_q, mepc_q, sepc_q, mcause_q, scause_q, trap_vector_base_o, epc_o. Expected guard: Writes to trap vectors, delegation, interrupt enable/pending, EPC/cause registers should be restricted to appropriate privilege level. Observed behavior: Read cases return supervisor and machine trap/interrupt CSRs. Write cases for MIE, MTVEC, MSCRATCH, MEPC, MCAUSE, MTVAL, MIP update state directly in the inspected snippet. mret/sret are set by csr_op_logic without visible privilege checks; epc_o selects sepc_q for sret and mepc_q by default. Path: CSR/trap-return instruction -> csr_regfile read/write and exception_ctrl -> trap_vector_base_o/epc_o/interrupt state. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unprivileged or insufficiently privileged software could redirect traps/returns or alter interrupt routing, enabling control-flow hijack or privilege-transition manipulation.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Complete exception_ctrl and csr_exception_o generation are truncated; no explicit privilege denial was observed in the provided snippets.

findings.summary: Security-sensitive peripheral/key storage paths are exposed through AXI/APB wrappers without visible privilege/security-attribute mediation. ROM2/FUSE has AXI-to-memory wiring with read/write request signals, and APB bridges expose PLIC, packet, AES, and UART buses; the observed ROM2 implementation permits reads and writes of key-containing secure_reg entries.
findings.vulnerability_category: MMIO/APB/AXI read and write. / ROM2 key storage/pkey_loc/jtag_key-related values, AES APB registers, packet APB registers, PLIC configuration. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: SoC AXI/APB bus master. Operation: MMIO/APB/AXI read and write. Object: ROM2 key storage/pkey_loc/jtag_key-related values, AES APB registers, packet APB registers, PLIC configuration. Expected guard: Security-sensitive MMIO should be mediated by address decode plus privilege/security attributes; secret reads and untrusted writes should be blocked or return safe values. Observed behavior: ariane_peripherals instantiates AXI/APB bridges and ROM2_FUSE wiring. ROM2 copies key constants to secure_reg on reset, allows writes to secure_reg when req_i && we_i, and returns secure_reg data on reads. No visible privilege/security/protection check accompanies rom2_req/rom2_we or APB bridge access signals in the inspected snippets. Path: SoC bus fabric -> ariane_peripherals AXI/APB bridge or axi2rom2 -> ROM2/AES/packet/PLIC peripheral register interfaces -> rdata/prdata outputs. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Any bus master able to reach these MMIO regions may read secret/key material or overwrite security policy/key state, including JTAG and AES-related values.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full ariane_peripherals address decode and any bus-level privilege attribute use are truncated; AES/packet internal register policies are not provided.

findings.summary: ROM2, described as holding keys, is writable and readable through ordinary request/write-enable signals, and includes a JTAG key entry. The JTAG key signal is wired from peripherals into dmi_jtag; no lock, read suppression, or privilege guard is visible on ROM2 write/read paths.
findings.vulnerability_category: Read/write ROM2/key-related registers and use key for JTAG auth. / ROM2 secure_reg contents, pkey_loc_o/key_reg_out, jtag_key input to dmi_jtag. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: SoC bus master and JTAG authenticator. Operation: Read/write ROM2/key-related registers and use key for JTAG auth. Object: ROM2 secure_reg contents, pkey_loc_o/key_reg_out, jtag_key input to dmi_jtag. Expected guard: ROM/key material should be read-only or tightly controlled; secret reads should be blocked; jtag_key should not be controllable by untrusted bus writes. Observed behavior: ROM2 secure_reg is initialized from constant mem on reset but is overwritten with wdata_i when req_i && we_i. rdata_o returns secure_reg contents. The mem includes a JTAG-labeled entry. ariane_peripherals defines jtag_key output and ROM2_FUSE wiring; dmi_jtag receives jtag_key and loads pass from jtag_key on reset. Path: AXI bus -> ariane_peripherals axi2rom2 -> rom2 req/we/addr/wdata/rdata/secure_reg -> jtag_key/pkey-related wiring -> dmi_jtag pass/jtag auth state. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A bus master may disclose or modify JTAG/AES/access-control key material and potentially control future JTAG authentication behavior or security policy.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact ariane_peripherals assignment from ROM2 secure_reg to jtag_key/pkey_loc_o is not fully shown in the provided observations.

findings.summary: Several reset defaults are safe, but reset/authentication interactions have concerning behavior: dmi_jtag loads pass from jtag_key only while rst_ni is low, dm_csrs response FIFO is not flushed except by dmi_rst_ni, and dmstatus is hardwired authenticated=1. ROM2 key registers are restored on reset but are writable during normal operation.
findings.vulnerability_category: Reset/clear/initialize permission-related state. / pass/auth state, DMI request/response state, dmactive/ndmreset, sbaddr/sbdata, debug_mode_q, CSR privilege state, ROM/key outputs. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Reset controller or external JTAG reset source. Operation: Reset/clear/initialize permission-related state. Object: pass/auth state, DMI request/response state, dmactive/ndmreset, sbaddr/sbdata, debug_mode_q, CSR privilege state, ROM/key outputs. Expected guard: Reset should place permission/security state in safe defaults: debug inactive unless authorized, no stale DMI/SBA data, CSR privilege safe, key/auth not permissive. Observed behavior: JTAG TAP state resets on trst_ni and csr/debug state reset clears many registers. csr_regfile resets debug_mode_q=0 and privilege to machine. dm_csrs PoR clears dmcontrol/sbaddr/sbdata/data and sets havereset. However dmstatus.authenticated is always 1, response FIFO flush_i is tied low and only reset by dmi_rst_ni, and dmi_jtag pass is loaded from jtag_key only during reset, making reset/key timing relevant. ROM2 restores constants on reset but permits normal writes afterward. Path: rst_ni/trst_ni/dmi_rst_ni -> dmi_jtag TAP/pass state, dm_csrs/FIFO/debug state, csr_regfile privilege/debug state, ROM2 key state. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Reset sequencing or deactivation may leave debug logically authenticated, retain queued response data until dmi reset, or restore/permit mutable key material, enabling transient/stale unauthorized debug access or data exposure.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Complete dmi_reset_o/dmi_rst_ni propagation and full dmactive clear behavior are truncated; exact stale-response exploitability is not fully proven.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
